home.title: SUDOKU
home.discard_message: Your progress will be permanently lost. Are you sure?
home.confirm_message: You have a game in progress. Starting a new game will erase your progress. Continue?

game.free_play_msg_one: 1 cell contains an error. Incorrect cells are highlighted in red.
game.free_play_msg_many: {{n}} cells contain errors. Incorrect cells are highlighted in red.

daily.title: DAILY CHALLENGE
daily.catch_up_blocked: A challenge is in progress. You can abandon it to play this one.
daily.out_of_window: This day is too old to be caught up (beyond 30 days).

stats.title: STATISTICS
stats.score_note: The score is the adjusted completion time: +10% per hint used, +5% per error. A lower score is better.

settings.title: SETTINGS
settings.large_numbers_desc: Enlarges the digits in the grid for better readability
settings.highlight_identical_desc: Highlights cells with the same value
settings.highlight_group_desc: Colors the area around the selected cell
settings.show_cell_errors_desc: Displays wrong numbers tried in each cell
settings.highlight_notes_desc: Highlights note candidates that match the selected digit
settings.blitz_mode_desc: Select a digit first, then tap cells to place it
settings.blitz_auto_select_desc: Tapping a filled cell selects its digit
settings.auto_notes_desc: Long press Notes to automatically fill or clear all valid candidates
settings.test_mode_desc: Shows the T button to test a hypothesis without risking errors
settings.haptic_feedback_desc: Vibrations when placing a number or making an error
settings.free_play_desc: Errors are not counted during play. They are revealed when the grid is complete.
settings.limit_errors_desc: Ends the game when the maximum number of errors is reached

hints.naked_single: Cell {{cell}} has only one possible candidate. Look at what its {{row}}, {{col}} and {{box}} have already placed: together, they eliminate every digit except one. Find which one.
hints.hidden_single_row: Scan {{row}} cell by cell: only one missing digit can go in cell {{cell}}. For each digit not yet in the row, count how many cells can still hold it — one of them has only one option left.
hints.hidden_single_col: Scan {{col}} cell by cell: only one missing digit can go in cell {{cell}}. For each digit not yet in the column, count how many cells can still hold it — one of them has only one option left.
hints.hidden_single_box: In the {{box}}, only one missing digit can go in cell {{cell}}. Check each digit absent from the box and find the one that a row or column eliminates from everywhere… except this spot.
hints.naked_pair: In {{group}}, cells {{cell1}} and {{cell2}} contain exactly the same two candidates: {{a}} and {{b}}. These digits are reserved for them — remove {{a}} and {{b}} from all other cells in the group. One cell should then be left with a single candidate.
hints.hidden_pair: In {{group}}, digits {{a}} and {{b}} only appear as candidates in cells {{cell1}} and {{cell2}}. These digits belong only to those two cells — remove all other candidates from them. Another cell in the group should then become clear.
hints.hidden_pair_cross: In {{group}}, digits {{a}} and {{b}} only appear as candidates in cells {{cell1}} and {{cell2}}. These digits belong only to those two cells — remove all other candidates from them. Then look in {{consequence}} for what this elimination unlocks.
hints.pointing_pair_row: In the {{box}}, all candidate positions for {{n}} lie on {{row}}. Since {{n}} must go somewhere in this box on this row, it cannot appear anywhere else on {{row}} outside the box. Find which cell on {{row}} (outside the box) is then left with no other option.
hints.pointing_pair_col: In the {{box}}, all candidate positions for {{n}} lie on {{col}}. Since {{n}} must go somewhere in this box on this column, it cannot appear anywhere else on {{col}} outside the box. Find which cell on {{col}} (outside the box) is then left with no other option.
hints.naked_triple: In {{group}}, cells {{cell1}}, {{cell2}} and {{cell3}} together contain only the digits {{a}}, {{b}} and {{c}}. These digits are reserved for those cells — remove {{a}}, {{b}} and {{c}} from the other cells in the group. New possibilities should open up.
hints.fallback_two: Cell {{cell}} has only two candidates left. To find which is correct, check whether either digit is already forced elsewhere in {{row}}, {{col}} or {{box}} — the other would then be your answer.
hints.fallback: No direct technique applies immediately. Cell {{cell}} is the most constrained in the grid with {{n}} candidates. Focus on {{row}}, {{col}} and {{box}}: look for a candidate that can be eliminated by an external constraint.

welcome.title: GGWP
welcome.p1: This mobile game is brought to you by GGWP, and this short promotional message will be the first and last disruption to your gaming experience.
welcome.p2: GGWP's mission is to offer quality mobile games — free, ad-free, and without collecting any of your personal data. This Sudoku is the first of many. If you are tired of apps that bombard you with ads, monetise your data, or push you to pay, you are in the right place. We will keep you informed when new games become available.
welcome.p4: Thank you for taking the time to read this. Enjoy the game!

rules.title: GAME RULES
rules.subtitle: HOW TO PLAY
rules.section_1_body: Sudoku is played on a grid of 81 cells, divided into nine 3×3 blocks. At the start, a few digits are already placed: these are your clues. Your goal is to fill every empty cell with a digit from 1 to 9, following three simple rules — explained below.
rules.section_2_body: On each horizontal row, the nine digits from 1 to 9 must appear exactly once. In other words, the same digit can never repeat on the same row.
rules.section_3_body: The same rule applies to vertical columns: every digit from 1 to 9 appears exactly once, never twice.
rules.section_4_body: Finally, within each 3×3 block, the nine digits must also appear exactly once. This is the least obvious constraint: always remember to check the block, not just the row and column.
rules.section_5_body: Pick a digit, say 1. Find every row, column and block where it has already been placed: it can no longer go there. If only one cell is left available in a row, a column or a block, that is where it must go. Repeat with each digit, and the grid gradually reveals itself.

techniques.title: TECHNIQUES
techniques.subtitle: DICTIONARY
techniques.intro: The techniques below are sorted by difficulty. Start with the Beginner and Easy tiers before tackling the more advanced ones.

last_free_cell.title: Last Free Cell
last_free_cell.definition: When a row, column or block has eight cells already filled, the ninth cell can only contain the missing digit. This is the simplest technique: just identify which digit is absent.
last_free_cell.example: On the highlighted row, eight cells are filled with 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8 and 9. The empty cell can therefore only hold the 5.

last_possible_number.title: Last Possible Number
last_possible_number.definition: For a given cell, look at its row, column and block. If together they eliminate eight of the nine digits, the remaining one must go in that cell.
last_possible_number.example: For the target cell, the row already contains 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and the column contains 2, 3. Every digit from 2 to 9 is eliminated, so only 1 can be placed there.

last_remaining_cell.title: Last Remaining Cell
last_remaining_cell.definition: Pick a digit and a unit (row, column or block), then ask: where can this digit still go? For each empty cell of the unit, if the digit already appears in the row, column or block that crosses it, that cell is eliminated. If only one cell remains, that is where the digit goes.
last_remaining_cell.example: We look for where to place the 5 in the top-left block. Three cells of the block are still empty. A 5 is already placed on row 2 (to the right of the block): it rules out the empty cell on that row inside the block. Another 5 is placed in column C (further down): it rules out the empty cell on that column. Only one candidate cell remains — that is where the 5 must go.

naked_single.title: Naked Single
naked_single.definition: A naked single is a cell in which eight of the nine digits have been eliminated by the combined constraints of its row, column and block. Only one candidate remains — that digit must be the answer. The technique requires pencil-marked candidates: the single then stands out at a glance.
naked_single.example: Look at the target cell on row 5, column E (the centre of the grid). Row 5 already contains 1, 2, 3, 4, 8 and 9 — six digits eliminated. Column E adds 3, 5 and 6; the 3 is already excluded by the row, so the new eliminations are 5 and 6. The central block contains 3, 4, 6 and 8, all already excluded by the row or the column. Altogether, eight digits are impossible: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9. The only digit that can still go in the cell is 7. That is the single candidate: place 7.

hidden_single.title: Hidden Single
hidden_single.definition: A hidden single is a digit that has only one possible cell left in a unit (row, column or block), even when that cell still carries other candidates. Unlike the naked single (which you spot by fixing a cell), the hidden single is found by fixing a digit and a unit, then looking for where the digit can still go. If only one option remains, place the digit — even if the cell looks ambiguous on its own.
hidden_single.example: Focus on column E and look for where the digit 7 can still go. In the top-centre block, a 7 is already placed at (row 2, column D) — it rules out the top three cells of the column. In the bottom-centre block, a 7 sits at (row 9, column F) — it rules out the bottom three cells. On row 5, a 7 is already present in column A — that row is excluded for column E. On row 6, a 7 sits in column I — also excluded. After all these eliminations, only one cell of the column can still host the 7: the target cell, on row 4. Even though the cell could in theory accept other digits, along the column 7 has no other possible location — that is what makes it hidden.

naked_pair.title: Naked Pair
naked_pair.definition: Two cells of the same unit (row, column or block) hold exactly the same two candidates, and nothing else. Since those two digits must distribute between those two cells, they are reserved for the pair. You can therefore remove both digits from every other cell of the unit — which often triggers fresh singles in cascade.
naked_pair.example: On row 4, two cells contain exactly the candidates {4, 7}: the cell in column B and the cell in column E. One will hold 4, the other 7 — the order is unknown, but both digits are locked to this pair. Consequence: neither 4 nor 7 can appear elsewhere on the row. Remove them from the candidates of the other empty cells. In column A, {4, 5, 7} collapses to {5} — a naked single appears. In column H, {4, 8} collapses to {8} — another single. The pair unlocks several cells at once.

pointing_pair.title: Pointing Pair
pointing_pair.definition: Inside a block, all candidates for a digit lie on a single row (or column). Since that digit must go somewhere in the block, it must go on that row (or column) — inside the block. You can therefore eliminate it from the rest of the row (or column), outside the block. The technique bridges two units: it uses a block constraint to free up a row/column constraint.
pointing_pair.example: Focus on the top-left block and look for where the digit 3 can go inside it. The third row of the block is already filled (three givens). The middle row of the block has three empty cells, but row 2 of the grid already carries a 3 in column H — so those three cells lose 3 as a candidate. Only the first row of the block remains, with two possible cells: columns B and C. That is a pointing pair — we don't know which cell holds the 3, but the block's 3 must sit on row 1. Consequence: 3 can no longer appear elsewhere on row 1, outside the block. Remove it from the candidates at column E and column F — two more cells freed.
